quick_facts.label: Occupational-standard reference
quick_facts.value: NSDC lists the apparel “Merchandiser” qualification pack AMH/Q0901 at NSQF Level 5, but labels it a retired occupational standard; use it as a role-description reference rather than as evidence of a current mandatory credential.

quick_facts.label: Core scope
quick_facts.value: The qualification pack describes the merchandiser’s work from order booking through shipment, organised around order booking, product development and pre-production activities.

quick_facts.label: Directly relevant undergraduate route
quick_facts.value: NIFT’s B.F.Tech (Apparel Production) teaches production planning, merchandising, work study and quality control, alongside knowledge of fabrics, trims and apparel manufacturing machinery.

quick_facts.label: Key operational documents
quick_facts.value: Role tasks include checking design briefs and tech packs, preparing bills of materials, costing samples, updating time-and-action calendars, maintaining trims/fabric/consumption worksheets, and supporting purchase-order and pro-forma-invoice processing.

quick_facts.label: Typical industry settings
quick_facts.value: The role is relevant across apparel manufacturing, export and buying-house/sourcing workflows; NIFT identifies merchandiser, sampling-room coordinator, product-development manager, production planner and supply-chain roles among apparel-production career outcomes.

quick_facts.label: Entry expectation in the retired QP
quick_facts.value: The 2019 qualification pack suggested graduate-level preparation and preferably one to two years of apparel product-development or production experience/internship; this is dated guidance, not a universal current hiring rule.

sections.heading: What a Fashion Merchandiser does
paragraphs: A fashion merchandiser converts a buyer or design requirement into an executable apparel order. In the NSDC apparel qualification pack, this starts with researching market trends, interpreting the design brief and tech pack, identifying quality, budget and timing constraints, and researching fabrics, trims and suppliers.

The role then coordinates development: selecting or updating vendors, collecting swatches, preparing the bill of materials, deriving initial sample costing, coordinating pattern-making and sampling inputs, and building a time-and-action plan. Before bulk production, the merchandiser maintains the pre-production file, convenes or supports the pre-production meeting, resolves cross-functional issues and hands over approved specifications. During execution, factory coordination, quality checks, shipment management and documentation are part of the defined scope.
bullets: Analyse buyer/client requirements, design briefs, tech packs, trend and target-market information.
Source and coordinate fabrics, trims and suppliers; maintain vendor information and development swatches.
Prepare or manage BOMs, consumption information, sample costing, production worksheets and time-and-action calendars.
Coordinate samples, approvals, pre-production readiness, factory follow-up, quality checks, shipment and associated documentation.

sections.heading: Entry routes from the listed education pathways
paragraphs: B.F.Tech (Bachelor of Fashion Technology), particularly Apparel Production, is a closely aligned route because NIFT covers apparel manufacturing, fabrics and trims, production planning, merchandising and quality control; NIFT explicitly lists “Merchandiser” among industry positions for this programme.

A B.Sc. in Fashion Design or Diploma in Fashion Design can be strengthened for merchandising by building demonstrated capability in garment construction, fabric and trim knowledge, tech packs, costing, sourcing, sampling and production coordination. These are the technical and operational capabilities reflected in the apparel merchandiser qualification pack rather than a statutory licence requirement.

Shorter, role-focused upskilling options also exist. For example, NIFT Bengaluru advertised a six-month Apparel Merchandising & Manufacturing Technology continuing-education programme for 2025–26; availability, fees and admission conditions are cohort-specific and should be checked directly with the provider.
bullets: Prioritise internships or project work with apparel manufacturers, exporters, buying houses, sourcing teams or product-development units.
Create evidence of practical work: a tech pack, BOM, sample-cost sheet, supplier comparison, time-and-action plan and sample-approval tracker.
Treat programme admission rules, course fees and employer qualification requirements as variable; verify them for the relevant intake or vacancy.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: Technical fluency matters: garment-construction processes, fabrics and trims, tech-pack interpretation, quality requirements, costing and pricing, vendor capability, production capacity, and compliance awareness are identified in the apparel qualification pack.

The role also depends on disciplined coordination. The same standard emphasises accurate written work, buyer/client communication, reading requirements, planning to deadlines, analysing trends and targets, problem solving and working across departments. The qualification pack also describes comfort with data analysis and MS Office as a useful personal attribute.
bullets: Apparel product knowledge: construction, fit, fabric, trims, testing and quality standards.
Commercial operations: BOMs, consumption, sample and bulk costing, supplier evaluation, purchase documents and delivery planning.
Execution control: tech packs, approval workflows, TNA calendars, pre-production files and shipment documentation.
Professional capabilities: precise written communication, buyer coordination, attention to detail, analytical thinking, deadline management and cross-functional teamwork.

sections.heading: Where the role operates
paragraphs: Fashion merchandising sits at the interface of design, buyers or clients, vendors, sampling, pattern-making, quality, production, warehousing and logistics. The pre-production and factory-coordination components of the occupational standard show that the work combines desk-based documentation and communication with operational follow-up across the production chain.

In India, relevant employers include apparel manufacturers and exporters, brands and retailers, buying houses, sourcing organisations and related fashion-business operations. NIFT’s apparel-production page names employers across manufacturing, exports, retail and fashion brands, while its placement material identifies export merchandiser, buying-house merchandiser, sourcing manager, sampling merchandiser, compliance officer and vendor-management executive as related fashion-business roles.
bullets: Buying houses, sourcing offices and vendor-management teams.
Brand, retail and e-commerce product or category teams.
Sampling, product-development, quality and supply-chain functions.

sections.heading: Career progression and adjacent roles
paragraphs: Progression is employer-specific rather than governed by a single national ladder. Experience in managing development-to-delivery workflows can support movement into adjacent or more specialised roles such as sampling coordination, product development, production planning or control, sourcing, vendor management, supply chain and quality assurance.

NIFT’s career materials list merchandiser alongside sampling-room coordinator, product-development manager, production planner, production controller, supply-chain manager and quality-assurance roles for apparel-production graduates. Its fashion-management placement material separately identifies export merchandiser, buying-house merchandiser, sourcing manager, logistics manager, sampling merchandiser, compliance officer and vendor-management executive. These are examples of adjacent destinations, not guaranteed promotions.
bullets: Early-career focus: sample follow-up, documentation, costing support and order coordination.
Specialisation options: export merchandising, buying-house merchandising, sourcing, sampling, production planning, quality or compliance.
Broader management directions: product development, vendor management, supply chain, category/product management or entrepreneurship.
